Description: Patterns that deal with object creation mechanisms, focusing on how objects are instantiated while hiding the creation logic.

Principles.Title: Singleton
Principles.Effect: Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to it.
BestPractices: Use for shared resources like Database connections or Logging services.
Ensure thread-safety in multi-threaded environments.
Be cautious of global state which can make unit testing difficult.
Consider using Dependency Injection as an alternative to avoid tight coupling.

Principles.Title: Factory Method
Principles.Effect: Provides an interface for creating objects in a superclass, but allows subclasses to alter the type of objects that will be created.
BestPractices: Use when a class can't anticipate the class of objects it must create.
Decouple the code that uses a product from the code that creates it.
Simplifies adding new products without breaking existing code.
Helps adhere to the Open/Closed Principle.

Principles.Title: Builder
Principles.Effect: Allows producing complex objects step by step. Enables producing different types and representations of an object using the same construction code.
BestPractices: Use for objects with many optional parameters or complex initialization.
Avoid 'telescoping constructors' (constructors with numerous parameters).
Improves readability of object creation code.
Enables creating objects in a specific state or configuration.

Principles.Title: Abstract Factory
Principles.Effect: Lets you produce families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes.
BestPractices: Use when your code needs to work with various families of related products.
Ensures the products you're getting from a factory are compatible with each other.
Avoids tight coupling between concrete products and client code.
Simplifies introducing new variants of products into a system.

Description: Patterns that explain how to assemble objects and classes into larger structures while keeping them flexible and efficient.

Principles.Title: Adapter
Principles.Effect: Allows objects with incompatible interfaces to collaborate by wrapping an existing class with a new interface.
BestPractices: Use when you want to use an existing class, but its interface isn't compatible with the rest of your code.
Acts as a middleware between two objects.
Converts data formats or method names to match expected interfaces.
Enables integrating 3rd party libraries without altering core business logic.

Principles.Title: Decorator
Principles.Effect: Allows attaching new behaviors to objects dynamically by placing them inside special wrapper objects.
BestPractices: Use to assign extra behaviors to objects at runtime without breaking existing code.
Provides a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality.
Follows the Single Responsibility Principle by dividing functionality between classes.
Ideal for implementing cross-cutting concerns like logging or validation.

Principles.Title: Facade
Principles.Effect: Provides a simplified, high-level interface to a complex library, framework, or set of classes.
BestPractices: Use to provide a simple entry point to a complex subsystem.
Reduces dependencies between the client code and internal technical classes.
Helps in layering a system by defining clear entry points to each level.
Prevents client code from dealing with the internal complexity of a framework.

Principles.Title: Proxy
Principles.Effect: Provides a substitute or placeholder for another object to control access, perform lazy loading, or add logging.
BestPractices: Use for Lazy Loading (Virtual Proxy) to delay expensive object creation.
Use for Access Control (Protection Proxy) to manage object permissions.
Use for Caching (Smart Proxy) to store results of expensive operations.
The proxy must strictly implement the same interface as the original object.

Description: Patterns concerned with algorithms and the assignment of responsibilities between objects.

Principles.Title: Observer
Principles.Effect: Defines a subscription mechanism to notify multiple objects about events that happen to the object they’re observing.
BestPractices: Use when a change to one object requires changing others, and you don't know the exact number.
Supports loose coupling between the subject and its observers.
Essential for creating reactive, event-driven systems.
Always handle unsubscription to prevent memory leaks in long-running apps.

Principles.Title: Strategy
Principles.Effect: Defines a family of algorithms and makes them interchangeable at runtime.
BestPractices: Use to switch between different variants of an algorithm within an object.
Eliminates massive conditional blocks (if/else or switch).
Decouples the logic of an algorithm from the client using it.
Enables adding new strategies without modifying the context class.

Principles.Title: Command
Principles.Effect: Turns a request into a stand-alone object that contains all information about the request.
BestPractices: Use to parameterize objects with operations and support undoable actions.
Enables implementing robust Undo/Redo functionality.
Useful for queuing, logging, or delaying operation execution.
Decouples the object that triggers the operation from the one that performs it.

Principles.Title: State
Principles.Effect: Lets an object alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing as if it changed its class.
BestPractices: Use when an object behaves differently depending on its current state and transitions are complex.
Eliminates large conditional blocks that manage state logic.
Encapsulates state-specific behavior into separate objects.
Keeps the context class clean and focused on managing the state transitions.

Description: High-level patterns that define the fundamental structural organization of a software system.

Principles.Title: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
Principles.Effect: Separates an application into three main logical components to help manage complexity.
BestPractices: Model: Manage data and business logic independently of the UI.
View: Focus purely on data representation and user interface.
Controller: Act as a bridge, handling user input and updating models/views.
Ensures high maintainability and parallel development of UI and logic.

Principles.Title: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)
Principles.Effect: Facilitates a clear separation of development of the GUI from development of the business logic or back-end logic.
BestPractices: ViewModel: Expose data from the model in a way that is easily consumable by the view.
Use Data Binding to automatically synchronize the View and ViewModel.
Reduces the amount of 'glue code' written in code-behind files.
Enhances testability of UI logic by decoupling it from the View.

Principles.Title: Layered (N-Tier) Architecture
Principles.Effect: Organizes the system into layers, where each layer has a specific role and only communicates with layers directly above or below it.
BestPractices: Presentation Layer: Handles user interface and experience.
Business Layer: Implements core logic and rules.
Persistence Layer: Manages database interactions.
Database Layer: Stores data. Use this for clear separation of concerns.

Principles.Title: Microservices
Principles.Effect: Structures an application as a collection of loose-coupled, independently deployable services.
BestPractices: Design services around business domains (Bounded Contexts).
Ensure each service has its own database to avoid tight coupling.
Use an API Gateway for cross-cutting concerns like auth and routing.
Implement decentralized governance and data management.

Description: Patterns that deal with multi-threaded design, ensuring safe and efficient execution in parallel environments.

Principles.Title: Active Object
Principles.Effect: Decouples method execution from method invocation for objects that each reside in their own thread of control.
BestPractices: Use to enhance concurrency and simplify synchronized access to a shared resource.
Invoke methods asynchronously via a proxy and a scheduler.
Enables long-running tasks to execute without blocking the main execution thread.
Ideal for UI applications that need to stay responsive during heavy processing.

Principles.Title: Monitor Object
Principles.Effect: Synchronizes method execution locally to ensure that only one method at a time runs within an object.
BestPractices: Use to protect the internal state of an object from concurrent access.
Encapsulates synchronization data and logic within the object itself.
Ensures thread-safe access to shared data structures.
Avoid deadlocks by defining a clear order of lock acquisition.

Principles.Title: Producer-Consumer
Principles.Effect: Decouples processes that produce data from processes that consume data using a shared buffer.
BestPractices: Use a thread-safe queue to manage the data flow between producers and consumers.
Allows producers and consumers to operate at different speeds.
Enables easy scaling by adding more consumer threads if processing is slow.
Handle overflow and underflow conditions using blocking or signaling.

Principles.Title: Lock Patterns
Principles.Effect: Mechanisms to synchronize access to resources in a concurrent environment, preventing data races.
BestPractices: Read-Write Lock: Allow multiple readers but only one writer.
Double-Checked Locking: Reduce overhead by checking conditions before locking.
Optimistic Locking: Use versions instead of locks for high-concurrency data.
Avoid 'Global Locks' to prevent massive performance bottlenecks.

Description: Patterns designed for the unique challenges of cloud computing, such as scalability, availability, and resiliency.

Principles.Title: Circuit Breaker
Principles.Effect: Prevents an application from repeatedly trying to execute an operation that's likely to fail.
BestPractices: Monitor the number of recent failures and 'trip' the breaker if a threshold is hit.
Provide a fallback response instead of waiting for a timeout.
Allow the breaker to 'half-open' periodically to test if the service has recovered.
Essential for microservices to prevent cascading failures.

Principles.Title: Sidecar
Principles.Effect: Deploy components of an application into a separate process or container to provide isolation and encapsulation.
BestPractices: Use for cross-cutting concerns like logging, monitoring, and security.
Ensures the main application remains focused on business logic.
Allows the sidecar to be written in a different language from the main app.
Optimizes resource usage by scaling sidecars independently if needed.

Principles.Title: CQRS
Principles.Effect: Command and Query Responsibility Segregation: uses different models to update and read data.
BestPractices: Separate the 'Write' path (Commands) from the 'Read' path (Queries).
Optimize the read database for performance (e.g., using NoSQL or Materialized Views).
Handles high volumes of traffic more efficiently than a single model.
Supports eventual consistency between the write and read data stores.

Principles.Title: Bulkhead
Principles.Effect: Isolates elements of an application into pools so that if one fails, the others will continue to function.
BestPractices: Partition service resources (e.g., thread pools, memory) per dependency.
Prevents a single failing service from consuming all resources of the caller.
Enables partial functionality even during localized outages.
Works well alongside Circuit Breakers for maximum resiliency.
